1: <header>Mitigating environmental impacts using net energy system in feed formulation in China's pig production </header>
Qile Hu a , Huangwei Shi a , Li Wang a , Lu Wang a , Yong Hou b , Hongliang Wang b , Changhua Lai a,* , Shuai Zhang a,*

<list>a State Key Laboratory of Animal Nutrition, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs Feed Industry Centre, College of Animal Science and Technology, China Agriculture University, Beijing 100193, PR China 
b 
College of Resources and Environmental Sciences, National Academy of Agriculture Green Development, Key Laboratory of Plant-Soil Interactions (Ministry of Education), China Agricultural University, Beijing 100193, PR China</list>
2:
<list>Using net energy system in pig feed for- mulation mitigated environmental effects. 
Using protein by-products in pig diet had superiority in environmental footprints. 
Feed crop production & manure handle contributed most emissions in pig produc- tion.</list>

1: Keywords: Formulation strategy Carbon footprint Nitrogen footprint Life cycle assessment Pig production system
2: As the world's largest pork producer, China is facing substantial environmental pressures caused by pig production and the relevant feed production. The net energy (NE) system is promoted as a new evaluation method to evaluate energy content in feed and energy requirements of pigs, but its application lacks of comprehensive and comparative evalua- tion from the environmental perspective. To identify influence factors and to develop mitigation strategies, the carbon and nitrogen footprints and land use (LU) of pigs (25-120 kg) in China were explored through scenario analysis and cradle-to-farm gate life cycle assessment (LCA). Functional unit (FU) was defined as 1 kg of live weight increase in pig. Among all the procedures of pig production, feed crop production and manure management were the principal contributors to the greenhouse gas (GHG) and nitrogen emissions. As for the carbon footprint, the GHG emissions ranged from 2.37 to 2.55 kg CO2-eq. FU-1 for scenarios using the NE system, 2% lower than that of the metabolizable energy (ME) system. Cottonseed meal-based scenario generated the lowest GHG emissions, and anaerobic digestion achieved the same effects as other manure management methods. As for the nitrogen footprint, reactive nitrogen (Nr) emissions ranged from 53.4 to 66.2 g Nr FU- for scenarios using the NE system, 4 % lower than that of the ME system. Peanut-based scenario won the lowest Nr losses. Moreover, arable LU ranged from 4.63 to 5.85 m² FU for scenarios using the NE system, 4 % lower than that of the ME system, and economic advantage by using the NE system was also proved. Sensitivity analysis and data quality assessment were conducted to quantify the uncer- tainties of the above models. In conclusion, the application of the NE system in feed formulation was an effective strat- egy to improve the environmental sustainability of China's pig production.
3: The global population is expected to exceed 9.5 billion by the year of 2050 (Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO), 2011), indicating rapidly growing demand for animal products in the next 30 years, which will result in expanded scale of animal husbandry. China has succeeded in feeding 1.4 billion people, about one-fifth of the world's population, with <10 of the world's arable land and <7 % of the world's water resources. China is also the largest pig producer and pork consumer in the world. Even under the influence of African Swine Fever in 2019, the annual swine slaughter still reached 540 million head, and the annual pork production reached 42.55 million tons, with the aver- age profit value of a commercial pig being 21 times more than that of 2018 (National Statistical Bureau of China (NSBC), 2020). However, due to the rapid development of the pig production sector and relatively poor management in China, pig production has greatly increased environmental pollution, especially through the waste of feed resources and improper dis- posal of manures and slurries (Zhang et al., 2021), resulting in great atten- tion on identifying and mitigating the related environmental burdens. Previous life cycle assessment (LCA) studies reported that feed production contributed to the majority of global warming potential and energy utiliza- tion, indicating that feed formulations are extremely important consider- ations in quantifying the environmental impacts of pig production system, while manure storage and handling caused direct emissions of nitrogen and phosphorous (Mackenzie et al., 2016; Monteiro et al., 2017; van Zanten et al., 2015; Andretta et al., 2018).

Energy costs account for %-70 of the total cost in swine feed, thus designing an optimal energy content is the primary goal in feed formula- tion. The traditional systems used to evaluate energy content in swine feed and energy requirements of pigs include the digestible energy (DE) sys- tem and metabolizable energy (ME) system, which represent the energy re- mained for digestion and that used for metabolism by pigs, respectively. However, the DE and ME systems systematically underestimate the energy content of feed high in starch or fat and overestimate the energy content of feed high in protein or fiber (Noblet et al., 1994). Net energy (NE) system removes the heat increment part from ME system, as a result, it is more pre- cise to evaluate the true energy value of the feed, especially for ingredients sourced from by-products rich in fiber and protein, and can unify the energy value of the feed with the energy requirements of the pig more accurately (Li et al., 2018). Previous studies also reported more economical benefits when using NE system for feed formulation (Garnsworthy and Wiseman, 2012). Considering the above advantages, the NE system is now gradually recognized and used in feed formulation in China's pig production. How- ever, the mitigation effect of NE system on environmental impacts on pig production has not been reported.

Carbon footprints originated from the concept of ecological footprints, which is now a popular characterization of greenhouse gas emissions in consideration of global warming potential (GWP) (Finkbeiner, 2009; Evandro et al., 2018). Carbon footprint is normally expressed in CO2 equiv- alent, and the three gases CO2, CH4 and N2O contribute 60 % and 5% to the enhancement of the greenhouse effect, respectively (IPCC, 2006). Nitrogen footprints is another footprint concept proposed after ecological footprints, which is mainly composed of food nitrogen footprints and energy nitrogen footprints, and can be calculated using the N-calculator model (Leach et al., 2012). Some previous studies have explored the carbon and nitrogen footprints of ruminant production systems such as dairy cows (Webb et al., 2020) and accessed carbon and nitrogen footprints mainly concerned changes between the input and output of substances (i.e., material flows) (Leip et al., 2008; Vries et al., 2011), but few studies took the integrated footprints of energy and substances into consideration. Moreover, the competition between arable land used for feed crops and that for food crops received a lot of concerns in recent years considering the lim- ited land resources in many countries such as in China. Possible solution, e.g. breeding for higher yield crops, diversifying diet formulation using local ingredients and by-products, and optimizing dietary protein utiliza- tion efficiency, were proposed to mitigate ecological burdens related to

land use in livestock production (van der Werf et al., 2005; Meul et al., 2012; Sasu-Boakye et al., 2014; van Zanten et al., 2015). Thus, effects on arable land use were also estimated in this study for the combined formula- tion strategies of using net energy system, and using by-products to partially replace soybean meal, which is the major protein source in traditional pig diets in China and mainly relies on import.

Therefore, the objective of this study was to introduce LCA to assess the carbon and nitrogen footprints as well as land use in a typical pig pro- duction system in China, differing in using ME system or NE system in feed formulation. Twenty-four scenarios were simulated, which contained 4 different by-product ingredients in diets as alternatives of soybean meal and 3 different manure treatment methods. The by-product ingredients included rapeseed meal, peanut meal, cottonseed meal, and extruded soy- bean. The manure treatment included composting, anaerobic digestion, and solid-liquid separation. The hypothesis that the utilization of the NE system in feed formulation could help to mitigate environmental impacts in pig production was tested.

7: In order to promote the optimization and development of pig breeding industry, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs (MOA) in China has guided to construct modern pig breeding industry in different regions, which was based on intensive farms using modern technologies including high potential breeds and commercial diets with an annual output of >3000 heads. In this study, we took such intensive farms as the focus of our research. The values of key performance indicators of such system (Table 1), particularly the average daily gain (ADG) and average daily feed intake (ADFI) of pigs at different growing stages, were sourced from the Nutrient Requirement of Swine in China (Li et al., 2020), which is the national standard (GB) for pig feeding in China openly-published by the MOA, and could represent the average performance of the intensive farms in China adequately.
8: To meet the nutritional requirements of pigs, different feed formula- tions needed to be designed at different growing stages, and all diets were formulated based on the Nutrient Requirement of Swine in China (Li et al., 2020) to ensure normal growth of pigs. Four growing stages were considered in this study, which were: 25-50 kg, 50-75 kg, 75-100 kg, and 100-120 kg of pigs. Since most of the feed are consumed during these growing stages, this study did not focus on the processes of

Average daily gain and average daily feed intake of pigs at each growing stage were shown in Table S1, and the total feed intake of pigs based on ME system and NE system were shown in Table S2. To compare the environmental impacts among different diets formu- lated based on ME or NE system in pig production, two paralleled diets were designed with the same ingredients combination. The control diet was formulated using ME values of ingredients and the corresponding ME requirements of pigs, while the experimental diet was formulated using NE values of ingredients and the corresponding NE requirements of pigs in each growing stage. To validate the superiority of the NE system in feedstuff utilization that were rich in protein and fiber, 4 extra plant-sourced protein ingredients were separately added into the basal diet as a replacement of soybean meal, creating 4 scenarios: rapeseed meal-based diet scenario, peanut meal-based diet scenario, cottonseed meal-based diet scenario, and ex- truded soybean-based diet scenario. Table 2 showed the compositions of these diets and the calculated nutrient levels of each diet.

11: The standard procedures of LCA modelling include: goal and scope def- inition, life cycle inventory analysis, impact assessment, and results inter- pretation (International Organization for Standardization (ISO), 1997). The goal of this study was to compare the environmental impacts of China's pig production using diets formulated based on the ME or NE sys- tem with different protein ingredients: rapeseed meal, peanut meal, cotton meal, or extruded soybean, which were all by-products of diverse oil crops. The function unit (FU) was 1 kg of live weight (LW) increasing in pig. The system boundaries, as illustrated in Fig. 1, was a cradle-to-farm gate system, indicating that the slaughtering process was not included.
12: Environmental impacts evaluation on the application of two energy sys- tems in pig production should form a cycle, which mainly included feed crop production and processing, transportation, pig housing, and manure management. Then the carbon and nitrogen emissions generated in the above processes were the elements that made up the carbon and nitrogen footprint. The carbon footprint was expressed in CO2 equivalent (CO2-eq) using the GWP of CH4 and N20, which equal to 25 and 298 times the GWP of CO2, respectively. The volatilized CO2 in feces was not considered. The nitrogen footprint included all nitrogen-related reactive nitrogen (NH3, N20, and nitrogen oxides (NOX) gases flows and losses except N2, expressed as Nr) (Leach et al., 2012). Direct and indirect N20 emissions were all in- cluded. The key emission factors and calculations of carbon and nitrogen footprints were provided in the supplementary materials.
13: Emissions generated from feed crop production and processing took the following procedures into consideration: agricultural materials produc- tion (e.g., chemical fertilizer, pesticide, and diesel and electricity for agricultural machinery), feed crop cultivation, non-plant feed production, primary processing and reprocessing of feed ingredients. This study consid- ered soybean produced in overseas countries (Brazil, the United States and Argentina) corresponding to the actual import situation of 2019 in China (i.e., 80.6 soybean imported) (China Agricultural Trade Development Report, 2020). Data related to feed crop domestically produced and the direct energy use were recalculated from the National Data Compilation of Revenue and Cost of Agricultural Products (2019). For the imported part, data from relevant studies and the Ecoinvent database version 3 were used as references based on the real production situation in those countries (Dalgaard et al., 2008; Sieverding et al., 2015; Silva et al., 2010; Silva et al., 2014; Wernet al., 2016). Concerning the non-plant feed ingre- dients production, emissions were quantified using two associated LCA

study concentrating on feed ingredients (salt, monocalcium phosphate, limestone and amino acids in the formulation) (Mosnier et al., 2011; Garcia-Launay et al., 2014). Emissions derived from the second stage of feed processing (reprocessing) mainly involved in the following steps: crushing, mixing, expanding, and pelleting. Data used here was 90 kWh electricity in total to process one ton of pig feed, which was estimated from a survey conducted in China (Liu et al., 2012).
14: Emissions generated from feed crop production and processing took the following procedures into consideration: agricultural materials produc- tion (e.g., chemical fertilizer, pesticide, and diesel and electricity for agricultural machinery), feed crop cultivation, non-plant feed production, primary processing and reprocessing of feed ingredients. This study consid- ered soybean produced in overseas countries (Brazil, the United States and Argentina) corresponding to the actual import situation of 2019 in China (i.e., 80.6 soybean imported) (China Agricultural Trade Development Report, 2020). Data related to feed crop domestically produced and the direct energy use were recalculated from the National Data Compilation of Revenue and Cost of Agricultural Products (2019). For the imported part, data from relevant studies and the Ecoinvent database version 3 were used as references based on the real production situation in those countries (Dalgaard et al., 2008; Sieverding et al., 2015; Silva et al., 2010; Silva et al., 2014; Wernet al., 2016). Concerning the non-plant feed ingre- dients production, emissions were quantified using two associated LCA

study concentrating on feed ingredients (salt, monocalcium phosphate, limestone and amino acids in the formulation) (Mosnier et al., 2011; Garcia-Launay et al., 2014). Emissions derived from the second stage of feed processing (reprocessing) mainly involved in the following steps: crushing, mixing, expanding, and pelleting. Data used here was 90 kWh electricity in total to process one ton of pig feed, which was estimated from a survey conducted in China (Liu et al., 2012).
15: In terms of transportation, two parts were considered: land transporta- tion and ship transportation. Assuming that the initial crops were all processed nearby, there was no transportation during this period, and the distance from the grain cultivation area to the feed mill was assumed to be 200 km by truck. The ship transportation distance of imported feed ingredients was estimated based on the distance from different countries to Tianjin port. The ship distance between the largest exporter (agricultural exports) in Brazil, the United States and Argentina to Tianjin port can be ob- tained from Netpas Distance (2019), which were 21,285 km (Santos port), 9795 km (Savannah port), and 21,781 km (Rosario port). Fig. S1 presented the location of these ports.
16: Emissions from pig housing and manure management were refined into pig production, indoor and outdoor storage, manure treatment and applica- tion for a more accurate and comprehensive calculation. Combined with the mainstream manure treatment technologies in China (Yuan et al., 2018), manure treatment was divided into three categories represented as treatment 1 (T1), treatment 2 (T2) and treatment 3 (T3), as shown in Fig. 1. T1 represented that slurry or solid manure were stored outdoors and returned to the field. T2 represented that slurry or solid manure was returned to the field after anaerobic fermentation. T3 represented that slurry were separated and composted before returning to the field. The proportion of the 3 manure treatments were 63.8 %, 24.8 %, and 11.4% in China, respectively, which were calculated based on data from surveys and published literatures, as listed in Table S3 (Long et al., 2021; Wei et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2008; Qu, 2012; Xuan et al., 2018; Jia, 2014). Carbon emissions were calculated based on greenhouse gas emissions including CO2, CH4 and N2O. Nitrogen emissions were calculated based on nitrogen flows and the Nr emission factors. Models used for calcu- lation and the calculating processes were briefly summarized in Table S4.
17: Economic allocation was the allocation of environmental impact among the main product and by-product based on their relative economic values, which reflects the environmental distribution more accurately (Guinée et al., 2004; De Vries and de Boer, 2010). In this study, the allocation factor (AF) of different kinds of feed ingredients was calculated separately. Alloca- tion factors were calculated according to Eq. (1), with AF of soybean meal, rapeseed meal, peanut meal, cottonseed meal and expanded soybean being 64 34 16 %, 20 and 100 % respectively (China National Grain and Oils Information Center (CNGOIC), 2019).

where AFi corresponded to the allocation factor (%) of the environmental cost of by-product i; M was the mass ratio of the by-product i; W repre- sented the market price of by-product i; Mn and Wn were the mass ratio and market price of n by-products of the same crop, respectively.
18: Effects of the energy system in feed formulation on the carbon, nitrogen, and cropland footprints of pig production in China were evaluated through scenario analysis. To identify influence factors, develop mitigation

strategies, and study the synergy among them, 24 scenarios were simulated throughout the life cycle of pig production (25-120 kg) in China, which was a combination of 3 factors with 2 levels, 4 levels and 3 levels, respec- tively. Specifically, the related factors included feed formulation method either based on ME system or on NE system, diet contained one of the 4

different protein by-products (rapeseed meal, peanut meal, cottonseed meal, or extruded soybean) as an alternative of soybean meal, and one of the 3 manure treatment methods (composting, anaerobic digestion, or solid-liquid separation as treatment 1 (T1), treatment 2 (T2), and treatment 3 (T3)).
19: Ingredients and nutrient compositions of different diets in four growing stages of pig production with 4 protein by-products included, formulated based on metabolizable energy system or net energy system (as-fed basis).

a Rapeseed meal-based diet. Peanut meal-based diet. Cottonseed meal-based diet. d Extruded meal-based diet. ME = metabolizable energy. NE = net energy. Premix provided the following quantities per kilogram of diets: vitamin A as retinyl acetate, 8250 IU; vitamin D3 as cholecalciferol, 825 IU; vitamin E as DL-alpha-tocopherol acetate, 40 IU; vitamin K3 as menadione nicotinamide bisulfite, 4 mg; vitamin B12, 25 ug; riboflavin, 5 mg; pantothenic acid as DL-calcium pantothenate, 15 mg; niacin, 35 mg; choline chloride, 600 mg; folacin, 2 mg; thiamin as thiamine mononitrate, 1 mg; pyridoxine as pyridoxine hydrochloride, 2 mg; biotin, 4 mg; Mn as MnO, 25 mg; Fe as FeSO4H2O, 80 mg; Zn as ZnSO4, 100 mg; Cu as CuSO4:5H2O, 50 mg; as KI, 0.5 mg; Se as Na2SeO3, 0.15 mg. All the dietary nutrient concentrations were re-calculated based on the diet formulation and the nutrient compositions of ingredients published in Nutrient Requirement of Swine in China (Li et al., 2020). SID = standardized ileal digestibility.

Feed cost was an important criterion to evaluate the economic impacts of feed formulations. The cost of feed formulated based on ME or NE system containing different by-products in this study were calculated using the annual market prices of feed ingredients in 2019 in China. The annual market prices of corn and the by-products were obtained from MOA in China, whereas the price of non-plant ingredients was collected from Feed Industry Information Network of China (https://www.feedtrade. com.cn/). Market prices were converted to US$ using the average exchange rate in China throughout 2019.

Premix provided the following quantities per kilogram of diets: vitamin A as retinyl acetate, 8250 IU; vitamin D3 as cholecalciferol, 825 IU; vitamin E as DL-alpha-tocopherol acetate, 40 IU; vitamin K3 as menadione nicotinamide bisulfite, 4 mg; vitamin B12, 25 ug; riboflavin, 5 mg; pantothenic acid as DL-calcium pantothenate, 15 mg; niacin, 35 mg; choline chloride, 600 mg; folacin, 2 mg; thiamin as thiamine mononitrate, 1 mg; pyridoxine as pyridoxine hydrochloride, 2 mg; biotin, 4 mg; Mn as MnO, 25 mg; Fe as FeSO4H2O, 80 mg; Zn as ZnSO4, 100 mg; Cu as CuSO4:5H2O, 50 mg; as KI, 0.5 mg; Se as Na2SeO3, 0.15 mg. All the dietary nutrient concentrations were re-calculated based on the diet formulation and the nutrient compositions of ingredients published in Nutrient Requirement of Swine in China (Li et al., 2020). SID = standardized ileal digestibility.

Feed cost was an important criterion to evaluate the economic impacts of feed formulations. The cost of feed formulated based on ME or NE system containing different by-products in this study were calculated using the annual market prices of feed ingredients in 2019 in China. The annual market prices of corn and the by-products were obtained from MOA in China, whereas the price of non-plant ingredients was collected from Feed Industry Information Network of China (https://www.feedtrade. com.cn/). Market prices were converted to US$ using the average exchange rate in China throughout 2019.

36: <header>2.8. Sensitivity analysis and data quality assessment </header>

Sensitivity analysis was carried out to check the robustness of the key parameters in the models and their influence on the results. Seven groups of parameters were considered in this analysis: (i) crop yield in feed produc- tion, (ii) the amount of nitrogen fertilizer input, (iii) feed conversion ratio, (iv) feed transportation distance, (v) CH4 conversion factor, (vi) NH3 emis- sion factor for manure management, and (vii) N2O emission factor for manure management. The emission factors obtained from literatures were defined as 25-50% (group (iv), (v), (vi) and (vii)), and parameters derived from survey or statistical data were defined as 5-15 % (group (i), (ii) and (iii)) (Hou et al., 2017; Velthof et al., 2012). This study adopted normalized sensitivity coefficients in sensitivity analysis to analyze the carbon footprint and the nitrogen footprint affected by the above parameters.

Treatment 1: slurry/solid manure- outdoor storage application
Treatment 3: slurry- separation- solid manure compost
The output: carbon and nitrogen emissions

a Date were obtained from the China Statistical Yearbook (National Statistical Bureau of China (NSBC), 2020).

Data quality assessment method (DQAM) was a novel hybrid environ- mental life cycle thinking tool, which could offer an accessible way to pres- ent results of the data quality assessment of environment models. DQAM scored data through two stages: (i) data robustness and evidence, and (ii) the level of confidence and consistency with existing literature (Weidema et al., 2013). The overall Data Quality Score (DQS) for Stage (i) and Stage (ii) were calculated using Eqs. (2) and (3) (United Nations, 2018; WRAP, 2019), and the two stages were combined to provide a simple visual presentation of the results: Excellent = DQS 2; Good = 2 < DQS 4; and Poor = 4 < DQS < 5. In the current study, the life cycle of pig produc- tion to assess carbon and nitrogen footprints were broken down into four processes: feed crop production, transportation and processing, pig housing and manure management, while those to assess the land use were broken down into three processes: yield, allocation coefficient and weight of crop. Within each of these processes there were underlying data and the life cycle inventory data that were assigned to each crop and activity.
where R was reliability, C was completeness, TeR was technological repre- sentativeness, GeR was geographical representativeness, and TiR was time- related representativeness.

where Q1 corresponded to Question 1: Do one or more data sources confirm the findings (within + 0%)? Q2 corresponded to Question 2: Do the key stakeholders/experts actively agree with the findings? Q3 corresponded to Question 3: Has feedback from the key stakeholders/experts been incorpo- rated? The pedigree matrix used in assessing each characteristic and the answers as well as scores for each question were referred to a new study (Salemdeeb et al., 2021).

40: Fig. 2 showed the carbon footprints (kg CO2-eq. FU-1 of 24 different scenarios in pig production in China, with 4 protein by-products included
Prim ary processing of feed ingredients

Fig. 2. Carbon footprints (kg CO2-eq. FU- characteristics of 24 different scenarios in pig production in China, with 4 protein by-products included in diets formulated based on metabolizable energy system or net energy system, and 3 different manure treatment methods. ME = metabolizable energy; NE = net energy; T1 = treatment 1, repre- senting that slurry or solid manure were stored outdoors and returned to the field; T2 = treatment 2, representing that slurry or solid manure were returned to the field after anaerobic fermentation; T3 = treatment 3, representing that slurry were separated and composted before returning to the field.

in diets formulated based on ME or NE system, and 3 different manure treatment methods. Cottonseed meal-based scenario owned the lowest GHG emission intensity compared with the other three protein by- products meal-based scenarios. The carbon footprints of NE system were lower than that of ME system under both T1 and T3 manure treatment methods, while were greater than that of ME system under T2 manure treat- ment method. The GHG emissions of the NE system, ranging from 2.37 to 2.55 kg CO2-eq. FU were generally lower than that of the ME system.

The proportions of the major processes throughout the life-cycle of pig production in GHG emissions, with 4 protein by-products included in diets formulated based on NE system, were shown in Fig. 3. The four pro- tein by-products meal-based scenarios embodied similar patterns in GHG emission proportions: feed crop production accounted for 45 % ~ 49 % of the total GHG emissions, ranking the first; pig housing came in second with 24 % ~ 26 of the total GHG emissions, followed by transportation and processing (19 ~ 21 %) and manure management took the smallest possession of carbon footprints.

43: The nitrogen footprint (g Nr FU-1) of all scenarios with 4 protein by- products included in diets formulated based on NE system and 3 different manure treatment methods of pig production in China ranged from 53.4 to 66.2 g Nr FU- which were lower than that based on ME system in gen- eral (Fig. 4). Peanut meal-based scenario owned the lowest Nr loss intensity throughout the life-cycle of pig production compared with the other three protein by-products meal-based scenarios, either based on ME or NE sys- tem. The results also exhibited that the nitrogen footprint of NE system under each manure treatment method was relatively lower compared with that of ME system, regardless of protein sources. Regarding to the ma- nure treatment methods, T2 led to the maximum value of nitrogen
losses regardless of the protein sources and energy systems compared to T1 and T3.

Fig. 5 illustrated the proportions of the major processes throughout the life-cycle of pig production in nitrogen footprints based on NE system. Similar patterns were observed in four protein by-products meal-based scenarios: feed crop production was far ahead, contributing 37% ~ 49% of the total nitrogen losses, followed by manure management and pig hous- ing. The extruded soybean-based diet scenario was slightly different in the nitrogen footprint of transportation and processing, which accounted for %, and was no longer the smallest proportion as in the other three protein by-products meal-based scenarios, which only accounted for 6-7%.

45: Fig. 6 showed the LU (m ² FU-1) of the cultivation area of crops needed to finish a 120 kg pig in each scenario based on ME or NE system. The ara- ble land use of ME system was generally greater than that of NE system on average, in other words, NE system was more land-saving. Data of LU with 4 protein by-products included in diets formulated based on NE system was distributed between 4.63-5.85 m² FU- and the average cropland occupation per pig of China's pig production was 5.17 m² FU-1 Among the 4 different protein source scenarios, peanut meal-based diet scenario occupied the lowest land area, whereas cottonseed meal-based diet sce- nario occupied the most land area.

the price gap between $1.249 ~ $1.308, regardless of the protein by- products included in diets. Feed price for pigs at earlier growth stage (lower BW) was more expensive. The cost of rapeseed meal-based diet was the lowest whereas the cost of peanut meal-based diet was the greatest.
49: <header>3.5. Sensitivity analysis and data quality assessment </header>

The uncertainty for carbon and nitrogen footprints of pig production in China using ME or NE system (expressed as coefficient of variation) was presented in Fig. 7. Changing the feed conversion ratio with + 15% caused 24% ~ 14 % changes in the results. Altering the crop yield with + 5 % resulted in % ~ 5 % changes in the carbon and nitrogen footprint. Changing N fertilizer %), or feed transportation distance ( 25 %), or NH3 emission factors of manure management 25 %) all led to both footprints changing with a range of % to %. Changing N2O emis- sion factors 50 %) and CH4 emission factors 25 %) of manure management caused an alteration of 2% to % and 1% to % in carbon footprints, respectively, while nearly caused no alterations in ni- trogen footprints for those two kind of factors.

Based on the DQAM, the graphical presentation of DQSs for Stage (i) and (ii) of carbon footprints, nitrogen footprints, and land use under the NE system were displayed in Fig. 8A-C, and the specific data including the calculation process were provided in Tables S6-S7. The DQSs illustrated excellent or good data quality (scores from 1.00 to 2.83) for all processes in terms of data robustness and evidence as well as confidence and consis- tency with current understanding and existing literature. All 7 processes were completely located in the green zone, which indicated that the overall quality of data used in modelling these processes were robust and consis- tent with published work.

51: 4.1. Analysis of emission sources of carbon and nitrogen footprints and land use

The LCA modelling is an internationally recognized and standardized method developed to evaluate the environmental impact of a product or service process qualitatively and quantitatively, then to guide further decision-making (Rebitzer et al., 2004; International Organization for Standardization (ISO), 2006). Previous studies have used the LCA model- ling to assess the environment impacts of livestock production, coverage of by-product assessment, effects of land use strategies and utilizing addi- tive combinations of growth promoting technologies on animal in different countries such as Brazil, Canada and China (Webb et al., 2020; Ali et al., 2017; Long et al., 2021). However, few studies have used LCA method to study the environmental effects of feed formulation strategies in livestock production. Thus, the current study focused on the environmental impact of energy systems used in feed formulation in pig production in China.

The feed crop production (including crop cultivation and agricultural material production) contributed the most to the GHG emissions in the 4 protein by-product meal scenarios, which was consistent with the previous reports that feed production was the main contributor to GHG emissions in the pig production chain (Cherubini et al., 2015; Groen et al., 2016; van Zanten et al., 2015). In two recent studies, Liu et al. (2021) reported the environmental burdens of small-scale intensive pig production system (around 500 pigs to be slaughtered annually) in China in the whole life cycle (Study 1), while Long et al. (2021) compared the environmental impacts among the smallholder pig farms (<50 slaughtered pigs per farm), medium scale pig farms (50-3000 slaughtered pigs per farm), and in- dustrial pig farms (>3000 slaughtered pigs per farm) in China in different provinces (Study 2). The scale of the pig farms analyzed in the current

Fig. 5. Proportion of major processes in the total nitrogen losses throughout the life cycle of pig production in China with 4 protein by-products included in diets formulated based on net energy system. NE = net energy.

study were similar to those in Study 1 as well as the medium scale pig farms in Study 2, and our results were also in consistent with those in the two studies: the carbon emission results fell within the ranges reported by those two studies (2.30-6.06 and 0.7-1.9 kg CO2-eq. FU-1, respectively), and the nitrogen emission results were closer to those in Study 2 (51-59 g Nr FU-1.

Manure management was the second biggest contributor to the nitrogen losses after the feed crop production as reflected in the current study, slightly inconsistent with some previous studies (Long et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020), which reported that manure management was the process that contributed the most to the nitrogen footprint. The possible reasons may be because nitrogen losses from SOWS and piglets were not considered

Fig. 6. Land use (m ² FU- of feed crops for pig production in China in each scenario with 4 protein by-products included in diets formulated based on metabolizable energy or net energy system. ME = metabolizable energy; NE = net energy.

60: <header>4.2. Mitigation potential for carbon and nitrogen emissions </header>

The current study explored the environmental impact during the whole process of pig growth from the perspective of animal nutrition, specifically, adjusting diet formulations using different energy systems and various ingredients, which was different from the previous analysis on pig growth process from the perspective of material flow (Ma et al., 2010; Wu et al., 2018). The patterns of carbon and nitrogen footprints in pig production was then analyzed from a new point of view to find out the major contributors to environmental emissions and the relevant strategies for mitigation.

Studies have shown that reducing the level of crude protein (CP) in diets by adding amino acids could reduce nitrogen excretion while maintaining pig growth performance (Le et al., 2001; Gloaguen et al., 2014). For every 10 g/kg CP reduction, the ammonia volatilization of pig manure and urine would reduce 8 % to %, and the input of pro- tein raw materials would also reduce % (Wang et al., 2018). However, the current study found that under the NE system, even if an ingredient with greater protein level was used, the related carbon footprints and ni- trogen footprints were still lower than scenarios used ingredients with lower protein level under the ME system. As a result, it could be as- sumed that the nutritional strategy combining NE system and low- protein diet should have positive effect on carbon and nitrogen emis- sions reduction in pig production.

The current study showed that using NE system in pig diet formulation could lower carbon footprints, % nitrogen footprints, and % land use per FU compared with the commonly used-ME system. Even though these mitigation magnitudes for environmental impacts were limited, with- out intuitive advantages as other strategies reported in previous studies (Long et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020), the quantitatively cumulative bene- fits would be significant considering the large scales of pig production sys- tem in China. Taken the year 2019 as an example, during which the annual pigs slaughtered in China reached 540 million head, with the upgrade of the NE system in pig diet formulation, the carbon emission could approxi- mately reduce 21.60 Mt., which was more than half of the total CO2 emission from electricity generation in Shanxi Province of China in 2018 (38.06 Mt. CO2) (Ren et al., 2021); while the nitrogen emissions could approximately decrease 1.47 Mt., which was more than a quarter of the NO emission from the power sector in China (5.52 Mt. NOX) (Chen et al., 2019).

In recent years, more and more studies have been conducted focusing on the crop-livestock integrative system, but most studies paid more atten- tion on planting and manure treatment stages, and there is a lack of consid- eration on the whole life cycle process (Wu et al., 2021). However, the methods and results from those studies were still worthy as references. Organically combination of the two different processes (crop system and livestock system) to find a balance between precise nutrition supply and en- vironmental impact would lead to great progress on sustainable livestock production. In the future, the parameters and algorithms of the carbon and nitrogen footprint assessment models during the entire life cycle of pig production established in this study could be further refined and vali- dated with data collected from practical production.

64: Uncertainties of the results in the current study mainly came from two aspects: data source and research scope. For the uncertainties in data source, most of the environmental emission parameters were derived from relevant foreign databases or published literatures, which, to some extent, may not objectively represent the actual production situation in China, especially for the small and medium-sized pig farms. For the re- search scope, the results of the integrated analysis in this study only consid- ered typical nutritional strategies, other factors such as pig breeds, pig house structure, and more complicated growth stages were negligible, which could also affect the results. For instance, in the study of Gao et al. (2019), live pigs in scaled pig farms in China were divided into 5 growth stages: pregnant sows, lactating sows, nursery pigs, growing pigs and fat- tening pigs, which were more precise considering their different nutrient requirements, while only 25-120 kg growth stages were concerned in the current study, with the calculation on SOWS omitted due to incomplete of re- lated data. Moreover, large quantities of rapeseed meal, peanut meal, and cottonseed meal were produced accompanied by the oil extraction process in small-scale oil processing workshops, in which the relevant data on energy input and output were not accessible, then may result in inaccu- rate environmental pollution effect estimation.

The current study may also have some limitations in carbon footprints and LU estimations, mainly because the influence of land use change (LUC) was ignored. It was estimated that approximately 8.707 billion tons CO2 was directly emitted in China in 2010, and the net absorption of CO2 by land use, land use change and forestry activities was 1.03 billion tons, result- ing in 7.678 billion tons of CO2 emission finally (Third National Communication on Climate Change of the People's Republic of China, 2018), indicating that LUC is also an influential factor in GHG emission estimation. However, the quantification of the land use change areas in the current study was based on annualized, retrospective data of the last 20 years (Poore and Nemecek, 2018), which may not fit the actual situation.

Under the influence of unfavorable factors such as fluctuation of inter- national crop prices, geopolitical conflicts, implementation of new national environmental protection policies, and pandemic, both pig stocks and pork production were affected to a certain extent. In the first three quarters of 2020, with the impact of COVID-19, swine stocks and pork production showed a year-on-year drop of 11.7 % and 10.8 respectively (National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC), 2020), and the spread of African swine fever during 2018 to 2019 further impaired the structure of the pork production (National Statistical Bureau of China (NSBC), 2020). Moreover, as the largest animal feed importer (Bai et al., 2018a, 2018b; Food and Agricultural Organization (FAO)., 2020), China had already cost an additional $5 billion in food imports from January to April because of the rise in international food prices (based on 2021 prices), thus the an- imal production system would be more vulnerable to market volatility and unexpected events. Bai et al. (2020) argued that China's food system (live- stock production sector) need revolution in the post-pandemic era, so it was necessary to be alert to the potential impacts in order to better cope with production shocks. As a result, techniques such as upgrading to the NE sys- tem, and increasing the substitution ratio for the imported ingredients using local ingredients or by-products in pig diet formulation, were pro- spective and meaningful strategies in maintaining the livestock production as well as food production sustainability in China.

67: Comprehensive and comparative evaluation of the energy system used in feed formulation in China's pig production was conducted from the environmental perspective. To identify influence factors and to develop mitigation strategies, 24 scenarios were simulated throughout the life cycle of pig production (25-1201 in China, which included formulation based on either ME system or NE system, 4 different protein by-products (rapeseed meal, peanut meal, cottonseed meal, and extruded soybean) in diets as alternatives of soybean meal, and 3 different manure treatment

methods: composting, anaerobic digestion, and solid-liquid separation. It turned out that the average carbon footprints, nitrogen footprints, and land use per FU of using NE system were 2 %, % and 4 % lower than that of ME system, respectively. Moreover, the application of NE system in pig feed formulation could lower the feed cost, and peanut meal partial replacement for soybean meal in pig diets could reduce the land occupation area. Feed crop production and manure management contributed the most to the GHG emissions and nitrogen losses. Nutrient strategies such as using NE system in feed formulation have great potential to improve the environ- mental sustainability of pig production.
